conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria, and then I'll ask you some questions about your circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the eligibility rules for Child Benefit. To help assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask: Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's helpful to know.

Next, I need to ask: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or has anyone else claimed it for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or does he live with you at home full-time?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually... he's been in local authority care for a couple of months now. He doesn't come home to stay with me - he's there full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I understand that can be a difficult situation.

To help determine your eligibility, I need to ask: Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local council is paying for his care while he's there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one final question to complete my assessment:

Do you regularly spend money on Alex - for example, on things like clothes, presents, food, or pocket money - and if so, approximately how much do you spend on him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm afraid I haven't been able to spend money on him while he's been in care. It's been really difficult financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I've now completed my assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit.

**Assessment Result: Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

While you would normally be eligible for Child Benefit as a parent living in the UK responsible for an 8-year-old child, the situation changes when a child is in local authority care. According to the rules:

- Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks
- Since Alex has been in care for a couple of months and the local council is paying for his care, you are no longer entitled to Child Benefit after the 8-week period

There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if Alex spent at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or if you were regularly spending money on his upkeep (such as clothes, presents, food, or pocket money). However, since Alex doesn't come home to stay with you and you haven't been able to make financial contributions while he's been in care, these exceptions don't apply in your situation.

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially during what must be a challenging time. If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex starts coming home for regular stays - you may wish to contact HMRC to discuss your situation again.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and has been in local authority care for a couple of months (over 8 weeks). According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. When a child is in care and the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance, the parent is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit after the 8-week limit. There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or if the parent regularly spends money on them. However, in this case, Alex does not come home to stay and the claimant has confirmed they have not been able to spend money on him while he's been in care. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a parent living in the UK with one child, Alex (aged 8). While they would normally be eligible for Child Benefit, Alex has been in local authority care for over 8 weeks, with the local council paying for his care. The claimant does not have Alex staying with them at home and has not been able to make financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep. Under these circumstances, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
